frames.caption: Diameter = longest path between any two nodes, counted in EDGES. Run a postorder Height() that, at each node, offers height(left) + height(right) as a candidate for the global best.
frames.note: The trick of every tree-DP problem: one recursive function does double duty. It RETURNS a height to its caller, and as a side effect it UPDATES a global `best`. The diameter never appears in a return value — it is accumulated across nodes. Start best = 0 and let the postorder pass raise it.

frames.caption: Node 1: height(L)=0, height(R)=0 → path through = 0 edges. Best stays 0. Return height 1 upward.
frames.note: A leaf has two null children, so height(left) = height(right) = 0. The path through it is 0 + 0 = 0 edges (it can't bend), and its own height returned upward is 1 + max(0, 0) = 1. This is tree-DP in miniature: combine the children's answers, update the global best, return a summary to the parent.

frames.caption: Node 4: height(L)=0, height(R)=0 → path through = 0 edges. Best stays 0. Return height 1 upward.
frames.note: A leaf has two null children, so height(left) = height(right) = 0. The path through it is 0 + 0 = 0 edges (it can't bend), and its own height returned upward is 1 + max(0, 0) = 1. This is tree-DP in miniature: combine the children's answers, update the global best, return a summary to the parent.

frames.caption: Node 3: height(L)=1, height(R)=1 → path through = 2 edges. New best = 2. Return height 2 upward.
frames.note: This is the heart of tree-DP: the two recursive calls already finished and handed us the heights of both subtrees. The longest path that BENDS at this node is height(left) + height(right) edges — so we offer that as a candidate for the global `best`. Then we return 1 + max(left, right) upward, because to our parent we are just a straight branch of that height. One value returned, another tracked globally — the signature move of these problems.

frames.caption: Node 9: height(L)=0, height(R)=0 → path through = 0 edges. Best stays 2. Return height 1 upward.
frames.note: A leaf has two null children, so height(left) = height(right) = 0. The path through it is 0 + 0 = 0 edges (it can't bend), and its own height returned upward is 1 + max(0, 0) = 1. This is tree-DP in miniature: combine the children's answers, update the global best, return a summary to the parent.

frames.caption: Node 8: height(L)=0, height(R)=1 → path through = 1 edges. Best stays 2. Return height 2 upward.
frames.note: This is the heart of tree-DP: the two recursive calls already finished and handed us the heights of both subtrees. The longest path that BENDS at this node is height(left) + height(right) edges — so we offer that as a candidate for the global `best`. Then we return 1 + max(left, right) upward, because to our parent we are just a straight branch of that height. One value returned, another tracked globally — the signature move of these problems.

frames.caption: Node 5: height(L)=2, height(R)=2 → path through = 4 edges. New best = 4. Return height 3 upward.
frames.note: At the root the two subtree heights are 2 and 2, so the path bending here is 2 + 2 = 4 edges — the largest yet. That fixes the diameter at 4. The winning path is 1 - 3 - 5 - 8 - 9: it climbs the left subtree to the root and descends the right. The bend need not be at the root in general, but here it is.

frames.caption: Height(root) returned; best = 4. The diameter is 4 edges: the path 1 - 3 - 5 - 8 - 9, bending at the root.
frames.note: Height(root) has returned and `best` holds the answer: 4 edges. The whole computation was a single postorder pass — each node visited once, O(n) time and O(h) stack space. The takeaway to carry to Balanced and Max Path Sum: return one quantity to the parent (height) while a global accumulator (best) collects the cross-node answer.
